text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia repairs. Today we're going to be talking about robotic tap ventral hernia repair with mesh. This is a case in a fifty nine year old man with a history of prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy who then had an umbilical hernia related to a twelve millimeter trocar at the belly button. The patient presented to the ED multiple times with pain. His past medical and surgical history will otherwise unremarkable his physical exam. He had a BMI of thirty-four and an incarcerated hernia at the umbilicus. No other hernias. He had a small diastasis of three to four centimeters, but was not bothered by this issue, so I felt that he was a great candidate for an RTAB ventral hernia repair. Imaging was an ultrasound, but I did not obtain any CT scans beforehand.

The surgeon uses robotic trocars to access the surgical site, reducing the incarcerated umbilical hernia with adhesions of the omentum to the hernia sac. The peritoneal flap is developed, starting in the falciform fat pad and extending approximately five centimeters from the umbilicus. The dissection is performed in the preperitoneal plane, avoiding the retromuscular space. The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to apply traction on the hernia sac, reducing it while carefully dissecting to avoid bleeding. A small hole in the peritoneum is noted for later repair. The dissection transitions to the contralateral preperitoneal area, creating a broad pocket for mesh placement.

The surgeon uses a V-lock absorbable suture to close the umbilical hernia defect in a transverse orientation, taking intermittent bites through the umbilical stock and fascia. The bedside assistant uses a DeBakey instrument to push the umbilical stock into view. The surgeon adjusts insufflation pressure to improve visualization during fascial closure. A small defect superior to the umbilical hernia is left open, with plans for broad mesh coverage. The Bard Soft Mesh is placed with the rough side facing the abdominal wall to enhance adherence.

The surgeon ensures the pocket is appropriately sized for the mesh, centering it and directing it cranially due to the patient's diastasis. Mesh fixation is performed using interrupted stitches with a 3-0 polysorb suture, securing the mesh to prevent rolling or clamshelling. The peritoneal flap is closed using a baseball stitch, starting from one apex and sewing left to right, with adjustments made for optimal hand positioning. The procedure concludes with the closure of a small fenestration using a V-lock suture.

Operation was great. Total console time was I think less than thirty minutes for this case. You see, operations, once you learn how to do them and get comfortable with them, can be very efficient. A lot of people when they're early in their learning curve can take quite a bit of time to perform these operations, but once you learn how to dissect in that preperitoneal space, you're able to get these done efficiently, and you're able to integrate higher volume case numbers for robotic tap ventral. Again, thirty-one minute total operative time, twenty-three minute console time, same day discharge, no narcotics used in a post-op period, great operation, a great option for patients with smaller defects.
